data.title: Sylvamo Corporation: Optimism In The Company's Performance Despite Free Cash Flow Decline In 2025
data.description:
Shares of paper and pulp producer, Sylvamo Corporation (NYSE:SLVM) are up 20.36% (YoY). SLVM announced its Q4 2024 and FY 2024 results where Q4 revenue was up 0.62%(YoY) at $970 million while FY 2024 revenue rose 1.4% (YoY) to $3.773 billion compared to $3.721 billion in the prior year. I am rating this stock as a hold due to operational concerns that may affect the company’s valuation in 2025. However, the company has made important strides since its spin-off from International Paper (IP) in October 2021 from a shareholder and business perspective, which I will also consider in the report.

Financial and Share Performance

Over the past three years, SLVM has outperformed the S&P 500 by 66.88% while IP has only gained 29.22% (in this period).

In the 12-month trailing (TTM) analysis, SLVM’s dividend yield of 2.41% has outpaced the sector average of 2.08% by 15.88% while the forward yield of 2.63% has exceeded the industry average of 2.10% by 25.61%. SLVM realized $632 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2024 and has recorded free cash flow (FCF) of $811 million since 2021.

However, SLVM reduced its adjusted EBITDA guidance for Q1 2025 to the range of $85 million to $105 million (a midpoint of $95 million) down 39.49% (QoQ) from $157 million realized in Q4 2024. There are operational issues such as the price decrease in paper in Europe and Brazil as well as Latin America’s adverse mix that may disrupt business and reduce SLVM’s earnings in 2025.

Valuation, Risks, and Future Perspective

Sylvamo is trading at a forward price-to-earnings (PE) ratio of 10.11X from a 5-year average of 7.23X and a sector median of 21.56X (TTM). It went public in October 2021 (after the spin-off from IP) opening at $29.55 per share and has gained 137.25% (as of this writing). The company reported an EPS of 1.94 as of Q4 2024 with FY 2025 consensus estimates ranging from 6.65 to 7.45. As of this writing, SLVM was trading at $69.95 meaning that the forward PE ratio in FY 2025 will range from 9.38X to 10.52X (giving a midpoint of 9.95X). I believe SLVM may trade at a high of $78.37 by the end of the year if it achieves the forecast EPS (on the higher end) representing an increase of 12.05% (from its current pricing) and a decline of 20.05% (from its 52-week high of $98.02). This metric shows the stock is undervalued at the moment.

While SLVM lowered its EBITDA guidance for Q1 2025 by 39.49%, QoQ (as outlined in the article), a look at the EV/EBITDA multiple supports my higher forecast for the stock. Firstly, SLVM's forward EV/EBITDA (taking into account the lower EBITDA guidance) stands at 5.78X against the industry average of 7.95X (a difference of -27.37%). Sylvamo's 5-year average for this metric sits at 5.09X, further supporting the fair valuation of the stock (based on its historical performance) despite the lower EBITDA guidance into Q1 2025.

In comparison, rival company Graphic Packaging Holding (GPK), with a market cap of $8.16 billion against SLVM’s $2.82 billion, has a 12-month trailing PE of 12.58X. For FY 2025, it provided an EPS guidance ranging from $2.53 to $2.78 (giving a midpoint of $2.66). With its current price at $27.17 per share, I expect the forward PE (by the end of 2025) to stand at 10.21X. These multiples mean the company will trade at a high of $28.38 representing a decrease of 7.56% (from its 52-week high of $30.70).

I would like to consider another company in the packaging segment, Ball Corporation (BALL) with a market capitalization of $14.59 billion. It gave a forward EPS of 3.52 for the FY 2025 against a forward PE ratio of 15.92X. I expect BALL to trade at $56.04 after attaining this EPS. While this future price represents an increase of 8.6% (from the current price of $51.59) it indicates a decline of 21.42% (from the 52-week high of $71.32).

In the wake of these assumptions, this industry is likely to face tumult as a result of a possible decrease in earnings in 2025. However, these companies may execute a turnaround to raise revenue. For instance, Sylvamo intends to invest $145 million to accelerate and modernize one of its paper machines and acquire a new replacement sheeter in South Carolina by the end of 2026.

SLVM’s cash from continued operations declined 6.94% (YoY) to $469 million in 2024 while capital spending increased 5.24% (YoY) to $221 million from $210 million in the prior year. The company is intent on growing its North American division, since it recorded a 19.40% (YoY) decline in CapEx to $54 million in 2024 from $67 million in 2023. Latin America had the highest CapEx in 2024 at $140 million (accounting for 63.35% of SLVM’s expenditure). Notwithstanding, the United States accounted for 53.78% of SLVM’s net sales in 2024 at $2.029 billion, while Brazil followed with $971 million in 2024.

I believe that by injecting $145 million into North America, SLVM intends to boost sales by more than 50% by FY 2026. There is also the 20-year partnership that will allow SLVM to outsource operations of its Eastover woodyard saving the company $75 million in CapEx (almost 34% of the current annual CapEx) over the next 5 years.

On the flip side, Sylvamo’s cash reserves have also declined 6.82% (YoY) to $205 million in 2024 from $220 million realized in 2023. Free cash flow also fell 15.65% (YoY) to $248 million in 2024 from $294 million in 2023. I will calculate the FCF yield to add color to the company’s valuation relative to GPK and BALL.

Bottom Line

I have rated SLVM as a hold, despite future operational concerns related to product pricing that may affect revenue generation. The company is preparing to ramp up production through 2026, especially in North America, which accounted for its highest revenue in 2024. I believe the company is undervalued in comparison to some of its peers, who have a negative free cash flow yield. Despite a decline in its FCF, SLVM's management has been able to handle CapEx and shareholder returns better than BALL and GPK into 2025.

data.title: Teva Pharmaceutical: A Risky Bet With Potential Upside
data.description:
Teva Pharmaceutical (NYSE:TEVA) has had a rough ride over the past few years. Debt, legal issues and pricing pressure in the generics market have weighed it down, keeping investors wary. But the company isn’t standing still. It’s cutting costs paying down debt and trying to shift its focus to higher margin biosimilars and specialty drugs. On paper that sounds promising. The real question is whether it’s enough to justify buying in now—or if patience is still the smarter play.

The stock looks incredibly cheap at the moment trading at 6.56x forward earnings, while the rest of the healthcare sector sits at 19.25x. At first glance, that kind of discount suggests a major buying opportunity. But a closer look shows why Teva hasn’t seen a meaningful re-rating yet. The company’s debt-to-EBITDA ratio has climbed to 40.5x which signals that even though it’s been paying down debt its earnings haven’t kept up. That’s a big problem especially with refinancing deadlines coming up in 2026. The market isn’t going to reward Teva until it proves it can grow its cash flow and keep its margins from slipping further.

Debt Reduction: A Step in the Right Direction But Still a Problem

Teva has made progress on the debt front bringing its total down from $61.7 billion in 2020 to $44.5 billion today. It also cleared $956 million in 6% senior notes and $685 million in 1.13% senior notes that were due in 2024. That’s a solid effort but it doesn’t change the fact that Teva’s leverage ratio has actually gotten worse because its earnings have declined.

The one advantage Teva has right now is time. There are no major debt maturities until 2026 which means the company doesn’t have to scramble for refinancing just yet. That breathing room could be enough to turn things around—if it can stabilize its earnings before then. If not, those looming debt payments will become a major overhang on the stock.

Profitability and Cash Flow: Margins Are Still Too Tight

Revenue in Teva’s U.S. business grew 4% to $8.03 billion driven largely by AUSTEDO and biosimilars but that growth hasn’t been enough to offset other pressures. Gross margin dropped from 55.7% to 54.6% showing that Teva is struggling to maintain profitability. At the same time selling and marketing expenses jumped by 11.8, a sign that the company is pushing hard to establish itself in new markets but also that it’s spending more to do so.

Teva’s operating cash flow took a hit falling from $1.368 billion in 2023 to $1.247 billion in 2024. Some of that decline comes from tax payments but the core issue remains: profitability isn’t improving fast enough. The company is investing in biosimilars and specialty drugs which have better margins than generics but those investments are still in the early stages. For now, it’s a waiting game to see if they’ll pay off.

Biosimilars: A Necessary Bet But Execution is Key

Teva has been pushing hard into biosimilars and it’s easy to see why. Unlike generics, biosimilars don’t see their prices collapse as quickly which makes them a more sustainable revenue stream. The company recently launched SELARSDI, its biosimilar to Stelara which gives it a foothold in the immunology market. It also has biosimilars for Prolia Simponi and Simponi Aria in the pipeline all awaiting regulatory approval. If these launches go well, they could be a meaningful source of long-term revenue.

One of the bigger moves Teva has made is rolling out SIMLANDI, a biosimilar to Humira which puts it in direct competition with major drugmakers. The Humira market is massive, but competition is fierce. Pricing and reimbursement negotiations will be key and success isn’t guaranteed. The biosimilars strategy makes sense but the real test is whether Teva can gain traction in a crowded field.

Why is the Stock So Cheap?

Teva’s valuation makes it look like a bargain. It’s trading at 6.56x forward P/E while the rest of the healthcare sector averages 19.25x. Its EV/EBITDA sits at 7.11x well below the 12x-14x range where larger pharmaceutical companies trade. On a price-to-sales basis it’s at just 1.12x compared to the sector median of 3.60x.

If Teva were a stable growing company, these numbers would scream undervaluation. But the reality is more complicated. The stock is cheap because of high debt shrinking EBITDA and uncertainty around its biosimilars strategy. Until Teva proves it can execute the market isn’t going to reward it with a higher multiple.

If the company can successfully grow its biosimilars business and expand margins a 10x forward earnings multiple would put the stock at $25-$30. But if profitability remains weak and debt remains a concern it could easily stay stuck below $20 or even drop toward $14 if things go south.

The Risks That Could Derail a Recovery

Even though Teva is moving away from generics that part of the business still makes up more than half of its revenue and pricing pressure isn’t letting up. The industry is becoming more competitive and the company’s gross margins are already showing signs of strain.

Debt is another major issue. Even after paying down billions a 40.5x debt-to-EBITDA ratio is still dangerously high. If interest rates stay elevated or refinancing in 2026 becomes more expensive it could create additional pressure on the balance sheet.

The legal overhang isn’t completely gone either. Most of the opioid-related lawsuits have been settled but Teva is still making settlement payments that drag on cash flow. The company is also under a Deferred Prosecution Agreement with the DOJ which means any new compliance issues could lead to hefty fines or restrictions.

The biosimilars push is promising but it’s not a guaranteed win. Teva has less experience in this market than some of its competitors and convincing doctors and insurers to switch patients to its biosimilars won’t happen overnight. If these products don’t gain traction quickly it could put more pressure on the company’s bottom line.

Is Teva a Buy? A Hold? Or a Stock to Avoid?

Teva isn’t in the free fall it was a few years ago but it’s still in a tough spot. The company needs to show consistent earnings growth before investors will take it seriously again. The stock is cheap but that’s because of real risks—not just because the market hasn’t noticed it yet.

At $16.78 per share, there’s upside if biosimilars start delivering and a price range of $22-$25 is possible if Teva can get its margins up. But there’s also real downside risk especially if earnings don’t improve before 2026 when major debt maturities kick in.

For investors willing to take on risk, Teva might be worth a small speculative bet. But for most it’s a hold until there’s more evidence that the turnaround is working. The company has potential, but it still has a lot to prove.

data.title: Evaluating Upstart: A Cautious Path To Investment In Uncertain Times
data.description:
Shortly after Upstart Holdings (NASDAQ:UPST) became public through an IPO (initial public offering) on December 18, 2020, it attracted considerable speculative interest because the idea of using artificial Intelligence ("AI") to determine credit risk intrigued individual investors. It was not long before the company began exhibiting many of the characteristics of a meme stock, which online trading platform Moomoo defines as the following:

A meme stock is a stock that gains popularity and experiences significant price changes due to social media activity, rather than the company's actual financial performance or business fundamentals. Platforms like Reddit, Twitter, and YouTube contribute to the hype surrounding these stocks, with retail investors amplifying price changes through collective action. Meme stocks are often highly volatile, experiencing dramatic surges and drops, sometimes without clear financial reasoning.

Upstart's stock rose 1,907% from its IPO (Initial Public Offering) price of $20 to reach an all-time high of $401.49 on October 15, 2021.

Some investors caught up in the frenzy behind the stock's rise failed to understand that using data-driven models for credit risk is not a new idea. First Marblehead became popular after its IPO in 2003 because investors were infatuated with the idea that it could use sophisticated models to determine student loan risk. However, the business collapsed during the "too big to fail" financial crisis. It turned out that First Marblehead's models worked great during good times. Still, in a black swan event financial crisis, other factors besides its ability to assess student credit risk undermined the business, which ultimately failed.

Upstart almost went down under similar circumstances to First Marblehead. When inflation began rising, peaking at 9.1% in June 2022, the Federal Reserve ("Fed") responded with its first interest rate hike in March 2022. Experienced investors understood that Upstart's AI algorithms had not undergone testing through a high interest rate environment and possible recession and quickly began selling the stock. Investor sentiment toward the company turned sour throughout the period the Fed raised interest rates. It reached an all-time low of $12.40 per share on December 27, 2022. The stock attempted to rally in 2023 and failed due to the probability of recession rising above 50%. Upstart has never weathered a recession, even a mild one, and there was no guarantee it could come out to the other side intact.

However, in mid-2024, the probability of recession began tailing off. The Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") of Upstart, Sanjay Datta, said the following in an interview with a prominent YouTuber around February 15:

The environment got really bad for awhile. I think that obfuscated a lot of the good work that I think we are doing internally and then it is as simple as it stopped getting bad. It stopped getting bad, probably a couple of quarters ago and as a result a lot of the good work that's been happening internally is starting to bear fruit.

There are two significant reasons to be bullish on Upstart. First, the economic environment has changed from a considerable headwind to at least being neutral, if not the beginnings of a tailwind. Second, the AI algorithms that the company uses are far more sophisticated than the data-driven models that First Marblehead used, which some probably wouldn't even consider AI under today's definition. Upstart's algorithms will likely have high accuracy in good times and even in high-inflation environments (it now has credit risk data in high-inflation environments). The most significant reason to remain cautious in the long term is that the AI-powered platform has yet to go through a recession, and there is still uncertainty about whether it will fare well.

This article will discuss Upstart's AI-powered platform and review its fourth-quarter earnings report. It will also examine its valuation, risks, and why I recommend it as a borderline Buy.

Upstart's AI-powered platform as a reason to invest

Upstart's Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer ("CEO"), Dave Girouard, left Google, the precursor to Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), in 2012 with a mission to improve access to affordable credit. He didn't fully understand how he would accomplish that task. Still, he believed he could use the same technologies and analytics that Google was developing to improve a system heavily reliant on FICO credit scores. Over time, the company evolved from traditional analytics that used past historical patterns to make future predictions to the most sophisticated AI that can quickly learn from new data and adapt autonomously. The company's 2024 10-K states:

Much of our historical growth has been driven by improvements to our AI models. These models benefit over time from a flywheel effect that is characteristic of machine learning systems: accumulation of repayment data leads to improved accuracy of risk and fraud predictions, which generally results in higher approval rates and lower interest rates, leading to increased volume, and consequently greater accumulation of repayment data. This virtuous cycle describes an important mechanism by which our business grows simply through model learning and recalibration. We expect to continue to invest significantly in the development of our AI models and platform functionalities.

During the fourth quarter 2024 earnings call, CEO Girouard introduced Model 19, which includes the Payment Transition Model (PTM). Previous AI models only looked at whether the borrower successfully paid the loan off or defaulted. PTM also looks at everything between when the lender originates the loan and its ultimate resolution. It includes tracking whether loan payments are late, paid on time, or become delinquent, the length of time the loan is delinquent, and whether the loan status returns to performing. These additional data points vastly improve the model's accuracy by helping it understand what happens to delinquent loans not charged off at a loss. CEO said about PTM, "We're often surprised by the increase in accuracy these types of innovations deliver..."

At the same time that the company improved its model, it also collected valuable lending data in an environment of high inflation and rising interest rates. It recently back-tested its new models and calibrations. On the fourth quarter 2024 earnings call, CEO Girouard said:

We recently completed a back test using today's macro handling tools applied to this period associated with intense macro volatility. We found that had we had today's tools throughout this time of volatility, we would have avoided 55% of the excess loan defaults observed in that historical period and would have returned to full calibration 12 months sooner. This analysis gives us confidence that we're making meaningful strides to improve the resilience of our platform through periods of economic volatility and bodes well for our future.

In other words, the company improved its AI models throughout the period of high inflation and rising interest rates. If it experiences a similar period again, it should be more accurate in predicting which loans are more likely to default.

As a result of all the model improvements, Upstart approves 101% more applicants while lowering the Annual Percentage Rate (APR), the yearly cost of borrowing money, by 38%. At the same, the loan platform significantly improved its ability to prevent loans from becoming a total loss.

This improved performance helps management attract more banking customers and increases loan origination. In the fourth quarter of 2024, Upstart's loan origination from banks and credit unions increased by 30% sequentially.

Company fundamentals

At the beginning of last year [2023], we set out some ambitious financial objectives. Turn around the growth trajectory of the business, continue to scale up our committed capital base, reduce the size and improve the performance of our own balance sheet, and return to adjusted EBITDA profitability. Our wish list consisted only of a stable macroenvironment. As we look back on the year [2024], we are pleased with our report card.

Source: CFO Sanjay Datta on fourth-quarter 2024 earnings call

During the earnings call, the CFO compared several of Upstart's fourth-quarter 2024 fundamentals to what the company felt was the low point of its business in the first quarter of 2024. Compared to the first quarter of 2024, it generates 44% more revenue on 86% more loans issued. On top of that, it is holding 25% less of the loans it originates on its balance sheet, reducing credit and interest rate risk. Last, the loans that lenders originate on its platform are once again profitable. The stock retreated in the previous two years because of these metrics' deterioration, and the improvement in these metrics is a welcome sight for investors. These improvements tell investors that Upstart has changed from a company struggling to survive into a company back in growth mode.

Management emphasizes revenue from fees because that is its core business. It generates fees from lenders using its platform, borrower referral services, and loan servicing. Upstart's fourth quarter 2024 revenue from fees increased 30% over the previous year's comparable quarter and 19% quarter-over-quarter to $199 million. CFO Sanjay Datta said, "This increase was partially due to the aforementioned model enhancements and partially due to a combination of lower UMI [The Upstart Macro Index] and last fall's rate cuts working their way into our platform pricing."

The company defines the UMI as an "estimate of the impact of the macroeconomy on credit losses for Upstart-powered unsecured personal loans." The December UMI of 1.40 continues to decline from its peak in July 2024, suggesting that macroeconomic headwinds are abating.

Loans originated on Upstart's platform are either retained by lenders, sold to institutional investors, or funded by Upstart's balance sheet. Holding loans on its balance sheet and generating interest is not part of Upstart's core business. However, when interest rates began rising in 2022, lenders began making fewer loans, and institutional investors bought fewer loans. As a result, Upstart began funding more loans using its balance sheet—the most significant reason the stock fell from grace in late 2021, as investors worried about potentially growing default rates.

The company's quarterly net interest income, interest expense, and loan fair value adjustments increased from negative $12.5 million to positive $19.7 million. CFO Datta said on the earnings call, "Net interest income was almost $20 million as we've now mostly worked off underperforming older vintages and as the falling UMI is increasingly reflected in improving fair value marks."

Upstart's fourth-quarter net revenue increased by 56% over the previous year's comparable quarter and 35% sequentially to $218.96 million.

The company's loan transaction volume increased 89% year over year and 31% sequentially to 245,663. Loan transaction volume in dollars increased 68% over the previous quarter and 33% sequentially to $2.1 billion. The average loan size increased 2.1% sequentially to $8,580. CFO Datta attributes the increase to "continued model improvements that allowed us to approve higher loan amounts." Its fourth quarter 2024 conversion rate, which measures how effective the platform is in converting potential borrowers into customers, rose 770 basis points to 19.3%.

The company's GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) operating loss was -$4.8 million, up from an operating loss of -$47.5 million in the previous year's fourth quarter. Investor sentiment surrounding this stock has been poor for several years because it generated a GAAP operating loss during difficult times.

Its fourth quarter 2024 GAAP net loss was -$2.8 million, up from -$42.4 million in the previous year's fourth quarter. Its GAAP diluted earnings per share ("EPS") was -$0.03, beating analysts' estimates by $0.35. The company's fourth quarter 2024 adjusted net income was $29.9 million, up from a -$9.7 million loss in the previous year's fourth quarter. Diluted adjusted EPS was $0.26, beating analysts' estimates by $0.30.

The company reports a non-GAAP contribution margin, defined as "revenue from fees minus variable costs for borrower acquisition, verification, and servicing as a percentage of revenue from fees." Management highlights this number because it highlights the efficiency of its AI-powered platform in proportionally generating more profit as loan volume increases. So, although the company currently produces a GAAP operating loss, the company's fourth-quarter contribution of 61% helps convince investors that Upstart can achieve profitability as the business scales.

Upstart's fourth quarter 2024 adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) increased 1.5% year over year to $38.8 million. Fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA Margin increased 18 points year over year to 18%. These numbers indicate improving financial health.

Upstart ended 2024 with $806 million of loans on the balance sheet, comprised of $703 million of loans held directly and $103 million from a securitization, from which the company retained minimal credit exposure. CFO Datta said on the earnings call:

The $703 million of loans held directly is down 28% from the prior year, but rose sequentially from $537 million in Q3, as the surge of borrower volume outstripped our expectations for the quarter. We view this to be a short-term timing issue. Rising borrower volumes are the signal for us to put in place the next set of capital arrangements, which we are now well down the path towards.

Management has stated an intention to reduce the loans on the balance sheet throughout 2025. The "next set of capital arrangements" without increasing loans on the balance sheet means expanding partnerships with banks and credit unions to originate more loans, sell more loans to institutional investors, or securitize more loans.

Upstart's cash and equivalents increased $445 million sequentially due to the proceeds from two convertible debt issuances. It ended 2024 with $788 million in cash and equivalents. The company's 2024 states the following about the convertible debt and warehouse loans:

Our convertible senior notes have an aggregate principal balance of $1,230.4 million and bear interest at a rate of 0.25% per year in the case of the 2026 Notes, 2.00% per year in the case of the 2029 Notes, and 1.00% per year in the case of the 2030 Notes, in each case payable semiannually. The 2026 Notes mature on August 15, 2026, the 2029 Notes mature on October 1, 2029, and the 2030 Notes mature on November 15, 2030, in each case unless earlier converted, redeemed, or repurchased in accordance with their terms...Our warehouse credit facilities, which mature between December 2025 and June 2028, allow us to borrow up to an aggregate of $475.0 million to purchase unsecured personal loans, $100.0 million to purchase small dollar loans, and up to $50.0 million to purchase auto loans.

Management guided first-quarter 2025 revenue to $200 million, above analysts' forecast before earnings of $187.57 million. If it hits its revenue guidance, the company will increase revenue by 56% year over year. Management forecasts a GAAP net loss of $20 million and adjusted EBITDA of $27 million, exceeding analyst estimates of $9.53 million.

Management's full-year revenue guidance of around $1 billion implies accelerating revenue growth of 57% from 2024. Analysts' estimates before earnings only called for $821.64 million. The full-year 2025 forecast also calls for break-even net income.

An analyst asked the company how much profitability Upstart will see during the year. CFO Sanjay Datta responded by saying the following:

I guess, we just reiterate that in 2025 will be in the ballpark of breakeven or at least breakeven. So I think we expect to be roughly in that ballpark, not dramatically ahead of it. In general, yes, I think that you could think of our profitability as improving with scale...as we grow, from the Q1 guidance we've given, which I believe is about minus $20 million, as the platform scales through the course of the year, you should see us then grow the profitability to the point where the full year will be breakeven to positive.

Once the company can achieve and maintain profitability, the market will likely value the stock more favorably.

Valuation

Upstart's price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 9.513, well above its three-year median of 4.208. Some may consider the stock overvalued based on where it trades above its P/S median. However, since investors anticipate the company coming out of a downcycle, they may have pushed the stock price up ahead of the revenue growth. It is feasible that its P/S ratio will decrease due to rising revenue growth over the next year or two without declining stock prices.

The following table compares Upstart's 2025 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth to several of its peers in the loan business. The market values profitable revenue growth more highly than unprofitable revenue growth. For instance, Fair Isaac (FICO) has the lowest revenue growth, yet the market values it the highest with a 2025 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth of 1.34. In comparison, Upstart has a meager 0.11 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth. The market has likely dinged it for being unprofitable. If Upstart reaches profitability by the end of 2025 and maintains profitability into 2026, the market will likely start paying much more for its revenue growth. So, this stock has an upside that comes with growing profitability.

Company Name 2025 forward P/S ratio 2025 forward estimated revenue growth 2025 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth TTM profit margin
Upstart 6.56 58.27% 0.11 -1.38
Fair Isaac 21.26* 15.86% 1.34 30.66%
LendingClub (LC) 1.51 18.89% 0.07 6.52%
SoFi Technologies (SOFI) 4.88 23.73% 0.20 19.09%
Enova International (ENVA) 0.85 16.58% 0.05 7.88%
Rocket Companies (RKT) 0.32 24.25% 0.01 -0.39

* Fair Isaac's fiscal year ends in September 2025, while all the other companies end in December 2025. So, the above comparison is not completely apples to apples.

Upstart's 2026 Price/Earnings-to-Growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.71 (forward 2026 P/E of 33.10 divided by analysts' estimated 2026 EPS growth), a sign of an undervalued stock. Typically, investors consider a PEG ratio under 1.0 as undervalued and will allow a stock to reach a PEG ratio of up to 2.0 before considering it overvalued. If Upstart sold at a PEG of 1.0, the stock price would be $93.64, a 40% rise over its February 25 stock price of $66.91. Suppose it reached a PEG ratio of 2.0; the stock price would be $187.29, up 180%. However, unless the market goes into a state of "irrational exuberance," investors shouldn't count on it reaching $187 any time soon, as this stock has several risks that may prevent the market from valuing it that high.

Risks

Despite the headwinds to Upstart's revenue growth dying down, the stock still has a relatively high percentage of its float short, signaling high bearish sentiment against the company. There are legitimate reasons that investors may want to avoid this stock in the short and long term.

The economy is a short-term risk. Although it has improved over the last six months, interest rates are still relatively high; the effective Fed Funds Rate is 4.33%.

With the current administration's hefty tariffs resulting in price increases, a potential government shutdown on March 14, potential government job cuts, and the threat that inflation could reignite, consumer sentiment is diving. The Michigan survey stated:

Consumer sentiment slid nearly 10% from January and fell for the second straight month. The decrease was unanimous across groups by age, income and wealth. All five components of the sentiment index deteriorated this month, led by a 19% plunge in buying conditions for durables, due in part to fears that tariff-induced price increases are imminent, said economist Joanne Hsu, director of the University of Michigan's Surveys of Consumers. Expectations for personal finances and the short-run economic outlook both declined almost 10% in February, while the long-run economic outlook fell back about 6% to its lowest reading since November 2023.

If conditions worsen, loan demand could soften, and headwinds to revenue growth may pick up again, potentially stalling Upstart's progress toward profitability.

A medium- to long-term risk is competition. Using AI to determine a borrower's risk is not a novel idea. Some of its competitors, such as SoFi Technologies and LendingClub, use AI and may have or will potentially create AI algorithms that are just as effective or more effective than Upstart's. If Upstart cannot differentiate its services, the stock's long-term potential upside may be less than the market thinks, and it could deserve a lower valuation.

Another concern is that although high inflation and rising interest rates have tested the company's algorithms, it has yet to experience a recession. There is a potential risk that the algorithms will fail during a recession. Even if Upstart's algorithms hold up, the potential for the credit market to lock up could have severe repercussions if it happens. When First Marblehead failed, it was not necessarily that its algorithms failed to work. It failed because the marketplace for loans seized up, so it could not sell securitized loans, and its guarantor failed. Something similar could happen to Upstart. Its algorithms may work beautifully, but the company may not survive all the falling dominoes if credit markets seize up as they did in the "too big to fail" era.

Upstart is a borderline buy

Economic uncertainty and the company's unprofitability prevent me from recommending this stock to risk-averse investors; they should avoid it due to the high risk.

However, Upstart has high upside potential. The company is expanding into other loan categories, such as Home Equity Lines of Credit (HELOCs), auto loans, small business loans, small-dollar relief loans, and T-Prime loans, significantly expanding its total addressable market ("TAM") to $3 trillion. With its current $628.83 in annual revenue, it is a drop in the ocean, barely penetrating its current TAM. Upstart's solutions have significant room to grow even in a competitive market.

If it can achieve and maintain profitability, the market will likely reward it by raising the valuation, as the company's forward PEG ratio suggests. At the current valuation, I would rate it a best buy if there were more certainty in the economic environment, but macroeconomic risks make me cautious. This stock is a borderline buy for a risk-tolerant investor who is mindful of this company's high risk.

data.title: The First Bancorp: 5.6% Dividend Yield And A Positive Earnings Outlook
data.description:
Since my last buy call in July 2024, The First Bancorp, Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:FNLC) has provided a total expected return of ~12%. As the net interest margin beat my expectations for the second half of 2025, I’m now expecting a higher margin for this year. Apart from margin growth, the net income will benefit from loan growth. Overall, I’m expecting an EPS of $2.71 for 2025, up 12% year-over-year. Compared to my last report, I’ve raised my EPS estimate because I’ve increased my margin estimate. The year-end target price suggests a 9.6% upside as of the writing of this report. Further, the dividend yield is quite high at 5.6%. Therefore, I’m maintaining a buy rating on The First Bancorp.

Stock Price Rallied Following My Last Buy Call

When I last wrote about The First Bancorp in July 2024, the outlook was quite bright as it seemed like we were entering an interest-rate downcycle that would last at least through the end of 2025. Low interest rates would’ve given a boost to the company’s key residential mortgage segment. Now, the Fed has paused its Fed funds rate cuts, and it seems like interest rates will remain high for longer than previously expected.

I adopted a buy rating on the company in the July report. Since then, the stock has provided a total return of 12.3%. Further, the earnings for the full year 2024 beat my expectations mostly because the net interest margin’s growth was above my previous estimates.

Cost of Deposits to Benefit from Last Year’s Rate Cuts

The net interest margin grew by 11 basis points in the third quarter and 10 basis points in the fourth quarter of 2024, far exceeding my previous expectations. The margin’s growth was due to a surge in loan yields while funding costs remained mostly stable.

Last year’s rate cuts could continue to benefit the margin this year because of the lagged re-pricing of certificates of deposits (“CD”). CDs carry high rates and made up more than 40% of total deposits at the end of December 2024, as per details given in the earnings release. Therefore, even a small drop in the rates on CDs could have a significant impact on the overall deposit cost, and consequently the net interest margin.

Management’s simulation model showed in September that rate cuts could significantly boost the margin in the second year of the rate cuts, as mentioned in the last 10-Q filing. (The 10-K Filing hasn’t been released as of the writing of this report, so the latest results aren’t available).

Overall, I’m expecting the net interest margin to grow by 9 basis points in 2025. Compared to my last report, I haven’t changed my margin growth expectation much. However, my average margin estimate is higher now compared to my previous estimate because the margin growth beat my expectations in the second half of 2024. So, I’m adding 9 basis points to a higher base now to arrive at my average margin estimate for 2025.

Outlook on Residential Loan Segment is Worse than Before

As expected, The First Bancorp’s loan book grew by 10.0% in 2024. The loan portfolio has grown by double-digit percentage points in each of the last five years. In my last report, I projected loan growth of 10.8% for 2025; however, now I feel that I will have to reduce my estimate because of my outlook on the biggest loan segment, i.e. residential mortgages. Residential loans and home equity together made up 36.8% of total loans at the end of September. I don’t have the updated loan composition for December 2024 as the 10-K filing hasn’t been released as of the writing of this report.

The biggest reason why I’m less optimistic about the residential loan segment than before is that the Fed seems to have paused rate cuts. Previously, I was expecting 50 basis points in rate cuts this year, but now I’m assuming that rates will remain unchanged. Borrowing costs are a key consideration for home buyers, and high interest rates can put a damper on demand. As shown below, the mortgage rates didn’t even decline much during last year’s 100-basis points rate cuts. Now that I’m expecting no Fed funds rate cuts this year, the mortgage rates will likely continue to remain elevated.

The First Bancorp mainly operates in Maine. The state’s housing sector has performed poorly in recent years, and total home sales volume has weakened. The following quote from the January 2025 Housing Outlook report by MaineHousing is of interest.

Rising home prices continue to be a primary concern for homeownership access in Maine. From 2020 to 2024, the median home price in Maine grew by more than 50% while the wages and salaries of Maine workers grew by less than 33.3%.

Considering these factors, I’ve decided to reduce my loan growth estimate to 8.2% for 2025 from my previous estimate of 10.8%. I’m expecting deposits to move in line with loans, so I’ve reduced my deposit estimate as well.

Raising the Earnings Estimate to $2.70 Per Share

The efficiency ratio worsened significantly in 2024 to 58.8% from an average of 50.9% from 2019 to 2023. (Side note: efficiency ratio is calculated as non-interest expenses divided by the sum of net interest income and non-interest income). The non-interest expense growth mostly came from salaries and employee benefits, as can be seen from the expense breakdown given in the earnings release. If the growth had come from a discretionary item like marketing, then I could’ve hoped that the expenses could come down in the future. However, salary and benefits expenses are less temporary in nature compared to marketing and other discretionary expenses. Therefore, I’m assuming that last year’s elevated efficiency ratio will continue through 2025.

While non-interest expense growth will restrict earnings, loan growth and net interest margin growth will lift earnings. Further, I’m expecting that non-interest income will remain unchanged from the last reported level. Moreover, I’m assuming that the provision expense will remain close to the average for the last five years.

Overall, I’m expecting The First Bancorp to report an EPS of $2.71 for 2025, up 12% year-over-year. In my last report, I estimated an EPS of $2.50 for this year. I’ve raised my EPS estimate because I’ve lifted my net interest margin estimate.

The biggest risk factor for my earnings estimate and investment thesis is the tariffs and the resultant uncertainty regarding the interest rate direction. The First Bancorp’s loan growth is significantly impacted by interest rates because of the residential mortgage segment.

FNLC has a Dividend Yield of 5.6%

The First Bancorp has been increasing its quarterly dividend every year since 2014. My earnings estimates suggest that the company could maintain its tradition in 2025 as well. However, out of an abundance of caution, I’m assuming that the dividend level will be maintained this year. The current quarterly DPS of $0.36 suggests an attractive dividend yield of 5.6%.

The dividend appears secure to me because my earnings and dividend estimate suggest a payout ratio of 53% for 2025, which is easily sustainable even though it’s a bit higher than the five-year average of 47%.

Maintaining a Buy Rating

I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples to value The First Bancorp.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with my EPS estimate gives a target price of $25.40 for the end of December 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with my tangible book value per share estimate gives a target price of $30.90 for the end of December 2025.

The average target price comes out to $28.20, which suggests a 9.6% price upside from the February 26 closing stock price of $25.73. Adding the dividend yield gives a total expected return of 15.2%.

In my last report, I adopted a buy rating with a December 2024 target price of $24.50. I’ve now raised my EPS estimate and rolled over my target price to the end of December 2025. Based on my updated total expected return, I’m maintaining a buy rating on The First Bancorp.
